hook: You already know that shapes have names — a square, a triangle, a rectangle. But what if someone handed you a shape with 7 sides and asked you to name it? Or asked whether a square and a rhombus are secretly in the same family? The rules for naming and sorting polygons go deeper than just counting sides, and without a system you'd be guessing every time. So here's the question: what exactly makes a polygon, and how do we classify all the different types?
concept: A polygon is a closed, flat (two-dimensional) figure made entirely of straight line segments that do not cross each other. More formally, a polygon is a closed plane figure formed by $n$ straight sides (where $n \geq 3$), with each side meeting exactly two others at its endpoints. Polygons are first classified by their number of sides, and then further classified as either regular (all sides equal AND all angles equal) or irregular.
anatomy: Every polygon is built from three kinds of parts: sides (the straight line segments that form the boundary), vertices (the corner points where exactly two sides meet — singular: vertex), and interior angles (the angles formed inside the figure at each vertex). The number of sides always equals the number of vertices and the number of interior angles — a pentagon has 5 sides, 5 vertices, and 5 interior angles. When a polygon is called regular, it means every side has the same length AND every interior angle has the same measure; if either condition fails, the polygon is irregular.

worked_example.expression_latex: \text{Shape: closed figure with 6 straight sides, all sides equal, all angles equal}
worked_example.description_latex: We start by counting the sides of the figure. Counting carefully gives us 6 sides, which tells us the polygon belongs to the family called hexagons — this is always our first classification step, identifying the polygon by its side count.

Now we check the two conditions for a regular polygon: are all 6 sides equal in length, and are all 6 interior angles equal in measure? The problem states both are true, so we move to the next classification.
Because both conditions hold, we classify this polygon as a regular hexagon. If even one condition had failed — say, the sides were unequal — we would call it an irregular hexagon instead.

Our final answer combines the regularity label with the name from the side count. Writing 'regular hexagon' communicates both the shape of the figure and the fact that it has perfect symmetry in its sides and angles.

partial_example.expression_latex: \text{Shape: closed figure with 5 straight sides; sides are NOT all equal}
partial_example.description_latex: We begin the same way we always do — by counting the sides. Count the sides of this figure and use the table of polygon names to identify what family it belongs to.
You found 5 sides — now write the correct polygon name for a 5-sided figure.

Good — it's a pentagon. Now we check the regularity conditions: all sides equal AND all angles equal. The problem tells us the sides are not all equal, so determine the full classification of this polygon.

Because at least one condition for regularity fails (unequal sides), the shape is an irregular pentagon. Notice we still keep the name 'pentagon' — irregularity changes the adjective, not the core name.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: \text{A stop sign has 8 equal sides and 8 equal angles. What is its classification?}
\text{Name the polygon that has exactly 4 sides. Then state whether a rectangle with sides 3 and 5 is regular or irregular.}
\text{A polygon has 7 sides. Two of its sides are longer than the others. Classify it fully.}
\text{A triangle has all three sides measuring 4\,cm and all interior angles measuring } 60^\circ. \text{ (a) Classify it by sides and angles. (b) What is the sum of its interior angles?}
\text{A tile designer uses a shape with 6 sides. Four sides measure 2\,in and two sides measure 3\,in. Is the tile a regular or irregular polygon, and what is it called?}

common_mistakes: Students often call any four-sided figure a 'square,' forgetting that square is only one specific type of quadrilateral — the regular one. The fix: always count sides first to get the family name (quadrilateral), then check regularity separately.

Students confuse 'equal sides' with 'regular polygon,' not realizing a polygon must have BOTH equal sides AND equal angles to be regular. A rhombus has four equal sides but its angles are not all equal, so it is an irregular quadrilateral — check both conditions every time.

Students miscount sides on concave or complex-looking polygons by skipping a side or counting a vertex twice. The fix: trace a finger around every segment of the boundary exactly once, tallying as you go, rather than estimating by look.
untested_variants: Finding the measure of each interior angle of a regular polygon using the formula $\frac{(n-2) \cdot 180}{n}$ — this lesson names polygons but does not calculate angle measures.
Distinguishing convex from concave (non-convex) polygons — a classification layer based on whether any interior angle exceeds $180^\circ$, which is not addressed in the practice problems above.
Classifying polygons given only a coordinate grid with plotted vertices, requiring students to compute side lengths with the distance formula before naming the shape.

Hierarchical classification questions about quadrilateral families — for example, explaining why every square is a rectangle but not every rectangle is a square — which require understanding subset relationships among special quadrilaterals.
